summary: Large mechanical formatting PR that changed explicit lowercase C float suffixes like `0.0f`, `1.0f`, and scientific/decimal `...f` literals to uppercase `F` across 130 `.c`/`.h` files. The PR body says it applied regex `\b[+-]?(\d+([.]\d*)?([eE][+-]?\d+)?)f\b` -> `$1F` in `**/*.c`, `**/*.h` as part of issue #487. It was closed without merge; a comment says this convention would be introduced more gradually as code is updated. The diff shows no semantic intent beyond literal-suffix casing.
what_changed: Replaced explicit lowercase `f` suffixes on single-precision floating literals with uppercase `F` suffixes.
Applied the transformation broadly across 130 files, with 1854 added and 1854 deleted lines, indicating line-for-line textual changes rather than functional rewrites.
Affected many fighter special-move source files, core fighter files, item code, vector/library code, map collision code, and ground/stage code.
The PR explicitly did not address implicit `f32` literals or `f64` literals, per the PR body.
The PR was closed without merge; the stated plan in the comment was to introduce the style more gradually as code is updated.
smart_moves: The author documented the exact regex used for the mechanical transformation, making the change auditable and reproducible.
The replacement targeted explicit `f32` suffixes only, avoiding broader reinterpretation of unsuffixed literals or double-precision `f64` cases.
Line-for-line replacement preserved surrounding code structure, which is important in a matching-sensitive decompilation project.
The follow-up decision to introduce the convention gradually avoided landing a very large formatting-only diff that would obscure future functional decomp changes.
decomp_lessons: In a decompilation repository, even harmless style-only sweeps can create large diffs that interfere with review, blame, and ongoing matching work.
Prefer applying formatting conventions opportunistically in files already being updated, especially when the change does not improve matching by itself.
When doing mechanical source transformations, record the exact search/replacement expression in the PR body so future contributors can verify scope.
Be explicit about what a sweep does not cover; this PR notes that implicit `f32` literals and `f64` literals remained unresolved.
Suffix casing (`F` vs `f`) is a source-style convention rather than a semantic change in C, so it should be separated from real decomp progress unless required for project consistency.
naming_conventions: Preferred convention proposed by this PR: use uppercase `F` for explicit `f32` floating-point literal suffixes, e.g. `0.0F`, `1.0F`, `-1.0F`.
The PR title and body describe these as `f32` literals, distinguishing them from implicit float-like constants and `f64` literals.
No new symbol, struct, function, or file naming conventions were introduced.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: This was a textual formatting pass, not an assembly matching tactic.
The transformation preserved numeric values and should not change generated code when the same compiler treats `0.0f` and `0.0F` equivalently.
The regex-based approach can be reused for carefully scoped mechanical literal cleanup, but should be staged to avoid massive non-semantic diffs.

review_feedback.feedback: The change would be introduced more gradually as code is updated.
review_feedback.implication: Broad repository-wide style sweeps were not preferred for this convention at that time.

review_feedback.feedback: The PR was closed with `merged_at: null`.
review_feedback.implication: Do not treat this large all-at-once replacement as an accepted merged precedent; treat the convention and gradual rollout note as the reusable lesson.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs referencing issue #487 or uppercase `F` float suffix convention.
Search for merged PRs that gradually replaced `0.0f` with `0.0F` in touched files.
Check whether project formatting guidelines eventually codified uppercase `F` for `f32` literals.
Look for remaining lowercase `f` suffixes or implicit `f32` literals in current source.
